entries.position_summary: The country supports negotiating a Fissile Material Cut-Off Treaty in Geneva. It views such a treaty as a necessary step to prevent future nuclear arms racing.
entries.quote: And, here in Geneva, we continue to advocate for the negotiation of a Fissile Material Cut-Off Treaty, a step needed to help stop future arms racing in its tracks.

entries.position_summary: The country urges immediate negotiations on an FMCT in the Conference on Disarmament, framing it as a crucial step to prevent future nuclear arms races.
entries.quote: It is time for negotiating an FMCT. We should take all possible steps in the CD to initiate negotiations on this crucial next step to prevent future nuclear arms races.

entries.position_summary: The country maintains a moratorium on producing fissile material for nuclear weapons and urges urgent negotiation of an FMCT in the Conference on Disarmament to prevent renewed nuclear arms racing.
entries.quote: We maintain a moratorium on the production of fissile material for use in nuclear weapons and continue to press for negotiation of an FMCT in the Conference on Disarmament, urgently needed to forestall a return to an era of nuclear arms racing.

entries.position_summary: The country supports negotiating an FMCT and urges China to join other NPT nuclear-weapon states in adopting a moratorium on producing fissile material for nuclear weapons pending such a treaty, framing this as part of fulfilling Article VI obligations.
entries.quote: Article VI. Pending such a treaty, we hope the PRC will join the other NPT nuclear- weapon States in adopting a moratorium on the production of fissile material for use in nuclear weapons.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports CTBT entry into force, backs its verification regime, funds and supplies IMS data, and continues its zero‑yield testing moratorium while urging China and Russia to adhere to similar moratoria and the DPRK to declare and maintain one, with greater transparency from all. It hosts observers at the Nevada National Security Site and commits to transparency in stockpile stewardship. The United States also supports negotiating an FMCT at the Conference on Disarmament in line with CD/1299 as a key next step toward disarmament.
entries.quote: 2000 Steps One and Two; 2010 Actions Ten through Fourteen: CTBT and Test Moratorium: On Steps One and Two, the United States remains committed to the CTBT’s entry into force and strongly supports the treaty’s verification regime. The United States is the largest financial contributor to the CTBTO PrepCom, provides significant voluntary contributions, and maintains high data availability from the IMS stations the United States is responsible for. The United States has not conducted a nuclear explosive test since 1992 and continues to observe its zero- yield moratorium on nuclear explosive testing. We urge the PRC and Russia to adhere to their respective zero-yield moratoria on nuclear explosive testing and the DPRK to declare and maintain such moratorium. We also urge the PRC, Russia, and the DPRK to provide transparency into their testing programs. For its part, the United States has continued hosting international observers at the Nevada National Security Site (NNSS), and we are committed to transparency in our stockpile stewardship and nonproliferation activities at NNSS and other nuclear security enterprise sites. 2000 Steps Three and Four; 2010 Actions Six and Fifteen: Progress on FMCT and Other Efforts at the CD: On Steps Three and Four, we support negotiation of an FMCT at the Conference on Disarmament as the essential next multilateral step toward achieving the ultimate goal of a world without nuclear weapons. Negotiations, which should take place in accordance with CD/1299, are long overdue.

entries.position_summary: The country supports arms control engagement, risk reduction measures, and advancing a fissile material cutoff treaty to strengthen the NPT. It also stresses the importance of negative security assurances and calls on all states to uphold the assurances they have given.
entries.quote: Steps such as these – on arms control engagement, on risk reduction, and on Cutoff – can help strengthen the NPT’s role as a positive force for restraint and diplomacy. So too do negative security assurances, and we call on all states to uphold the assurances they have given.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports negotiating an FMCT, has engaged in UNGA FMCT initiatives, and maintains a unilateral moratorium on producing fissile material for weapons while urging China to follow suit. It cites irreversible disarmament steps—dismantling plutonium production reactors, stockpile reductions via arms control with Russia, and fissile material disposition—and endorses the principles of irreversibility, verifiability, and transparency. The U.S. advances verification capabilities through the IPNDV, Quad Nuclear Verification Partnership, and UN GGE.
entries.quote: We will also engage constructively in the other subsidiary bodies, including on the Cessation of the nuclear arms race and nuclear disarmament. In addition to our efforts at the CD, the United States actively participated in two UN General Assembly FMCT initiatives in 2014-2015 and 2017-2018 that we believe laid important groundwork for future FMCT negotiations. We continue to maintain our unilateral moratorium on the production of fissile material for use in nuclear weapons and urge the PRC – the only Nuclear Weapon State that has not done the same – to join us. 2000 Steps Five and Thirteen; 2010 Actions Two and Eighteen: Irreversibility and Further Development of Verification Capabilities: On Steps Five and Thirteen, the United States has undertaken important steps to support the principal of irreversibility, including through irreversible dismantlement of all weapons-grade plutonium production reactors, stockpile reductions through arms control treaties with Russia, disposition of fissile material from nuclear weapons, and other efforts. We also support language from the 2022 RevCon Draft Final document that acknowledges the mutually reinforcing principles of irreversibility, verifiability, and transparency. Active U.S. engagement in the IPNDV, Quad Nuclear Verification Partnership, and UN GGE underscore our commitment to engaging a broad set of countries on the development of future tools for arms control that support irreversibility, verifiability, and transparency in nuclear disarmament. We also welcome the group statement on irreversibility in nuclear disarmament delivered by Norway,

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes high transparency standards for its nuclear policies and supports incorporating structured dialogue, such as national reports, into the NPT review process, regretting that this was blocked. It calls for the urgent negotiation of a Fissile Material Cutoff Treaty to prevent a renewed nuclear arms race and lay groundwork for further progress.
entries.quote: It is why the United States prioritizes high standards for transparency of our nuclear policies and programs. We welcome discussion on our national report, and deeply regret that certain parties prevented the Working Group from adopting such structured dialogue a regular part of the NPT review process.] It is also why we believe the time has come for a long overdue – and urgently needed – Fissile Material Cutoff Treaty. Without it, we risk a return to an era of nuclear arms racing. With it, we can set a better foundation for progress on

entries.position_summary: The United States criticizes China’s rapid, opaque expansion of its nuclear arsenal, citing new ICBM silos, delivery systems, and ongoing production of fissile material, with projections of 1,000+ warheads by 2030. It argues China’s buildup contradicts its stated policies of minimum deterrence and No First Use and contrasts with other NPT nuclear-weapon states’ behavior. The U.S. asserts it acts responsibly as a nuclear-weapon state under the NPT.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, the United States is committed to responsible behavior as a nuclear-weapon State under the NPT. Not all NPT nuclear-weapon States have done the same. We continue to be deeply concerned by China’s rapid, opaque, and destabilizing expansion of its nuclear arsenal, including the construction of hundreds of new ICBM silos and the development of new delivery systems. We assess that China currently has 600 operational nuclear weapons, which will increase to over 1,000 by the year 2030, and that it is actively producing new fissile material for use in its growing nuclear arsenal, unlike the other four nuclear-weapon states. China’s activities indicate it seeks the ability to inflict far greater levels of overwhelming damage to an adversary in a nuclear exchange than it has historically deemed necessary, and all while professing to apply “minimum deterrence” and “No First Use” in its nuclear authorities.

entries.position_summary: The United States is willing to pursue negotiations in the Conference on Disarmament on effective international arrangements providing security assurances to non-nuclear-weapon states. It also supports commencing negotiations on a Fissile Material Cutoff Treaty as the next logical disarmament step and urges leveraging the CD’s work program to advance these talks.
entries.quote: At the same time, and in a spirit of compromise, the United States is prepared to discuss the establishment of an ad hoc committee in the CD to negotiate on effective international arrangements to assure non-nuclear-weapon states against the use or threat of use of nuclear weapons, in conjunction with commencing negotiations on a Fissile Material Cutoff Treaty, which we have long viewed as the next logical step in disarmament. With the adoption of a Decision of Work in the CD and the prospect of sustained Subsidiary Body work in 2025, we should take this charge seriously and press forward with creativity and flexibility.

entries.position_summary: Supports an immediate global moratorium on producing fissile material for nuclear weapons pending conclusion of an FMCT, and calls for transparency in civilian production of weapons-usable materials, likely through strengthened safeguards and reporting. The aim is to curb weapons-usable material accumulation and build confidence while negotiations proceed.
entries.quote: Pending that negotiation, it is time for all states to declare moratoria on the production of fissile material for use in nuclear weapons and other nuclear explosive devices and to provide transparency into their civil production of weapons usable material.

entries.position_summary: The state frames its approach around the 2000 Thirteen Steps and the 2010 Action Plan, prioritizing bans/moratoria on nuclear testing, halting and disposing of fissile material production, and ensuring transparency and verification as core disarmament elements. It signals support for CTBT and FMCT-related measures and broader Article VI implementation.
entries.quote: I will frame my abridged remarks around the Thirteen Steps agreed to at the 2000 NPT Review Conference, which addressed nuclear testing, fissile material production and disposition, irreversibility, transparency, and verification, among other priorities, as key elements of disarmament. Our efforts also line up well with the relevant actions from the 2010 Action Plan. Our full statement will be available online.
